title: Origin of the Zwin and the island of Wulpen
text: As a result of flooding due to rising sea levels between the 4th and 7th centuries, the inlet known as the Zwin was formed, which is referred to as Sincfal in the oldest historical documents. According to the Lex Frisionum (8th century), the southern boundary of the land of the Frisians was located by this water. It is likely that the formation of the Zwin separated Wulpen from the mainland as an island and that the area of the later island of Cadzand became flooded. The island of Wulpen was presumably located to the north of the later island of Cadzand. The exact location has not been established.

metadata.source: Historisch overzicht van de Dorpskerk te Cadzand

title: Willibrord preached the gospel on the island of Wulpen (690).
text: Around the year 690, Willibrord preached the gospel on the island and presumably went from there to Walcheren.

metadata.source: Historisch overzicht van de Dorpskerk te Cadzand

title: First human settlement of the Flemish coastal plain and St. Peter's Abbey
text: At the beginning of the century, the first indications of human habitation of the Flemish coastal plain can be found in the archives. Grants of land in the coastal region to the Ghent St. Peter's Abbey are recorded in the Liber traditionum, which dates from 941. These were primarily marshy meadows, the size of which was indicated by the number of sheep that could graze there. For example, in the year 737: a sheep pasture at Grifnigas (near St-Anna ter Muiden), which could feed thirty sheep in the summer.

metadata.source: Historisch overzicht van de Dorpskerk te Cadzand

title: The Normans on the island of Wulpen and the Kudrun saga
text: The announcements from the St. Peter's Abbey are interrupted by the arrival of the Normans. There is no information from the years between 840 and 922. During their raids along the North Sea coast around the middle of the 9th century, the Normans also settled on the island of Wulpen. The famous Kudrun saga recounts that King Hette fought against the robbers of Kudrun on an island called Wülpensant.

metadata.source: Historisch overzicht van de Dorpskerk te Cadzand

title: Foundation of the castles Rodenburg and Oostburg (9th century)
text: It is believed that in the 9th century, in order to protect themselves against both the Normans and the sea, the castles Rodenburg (later called Aardenburg) and Oostburg were founded.

metadata.source: Historisch overzicht van de Dorpskerk te Cadzand

title:
Historisch fragment: ligging en vroegere omvang van Cadzand

Cadzand, gelegen aan de kust van Zeeland, was in vroegere tijden een belangrijke plaats. De ligging aan de monding van de Westerschelde maakte het tot een strategisch punt voor handel en scheepvaart. In de middeleeuwen was Cadzand een bloeiende stad, die deel uitmaakte van het graafschap Vlaanderen. 

De omvang van Cadzand in die tijd was aanzienlijk. Het had een eigen haven, die drukbezocht werd door handelaren uit verschillende delen van Europa. De stad was omringd door wallen en had verschillende poorten die toegang gaven tot de binnenstad. 

In de 14e en 15e eeuw kende Cadzand een periode van groei, maar door verzanding en de opkomst van andere havens, zoals die van Brugge, begon de stad in belang af te nemen. De bevolking nam af en veel gebouwen raakten in verval. 

Tegen de 17e eeuw was de omvang van Cadzand aanzienlijk verminderd, en de stad was niet meer de bloeiende handelsplaats die het ooit was. De veranderingen in de zeespiegel en de voortdurende strijd tegen het water hebben ook bijgedragen aan de achteruitgang van deze eens zo belangrijke plaats. 

Cadzand is tegenwoordig nog steeds een dorp, maar de sporen van zijn rijke geschiedenis zijn nog steeds zichtbaar in de omgeving.
text: CADZAND, located on the west side of the Hondt or Western Scheldt opposite Vlissingen; and separated from all of Flanders, of which it is nevertheless a part, by the Zwin (the port of Sluys). Some say that it used to be much larger and had a town with several wealthy villages; but that the wild waves have gradually, over the course of many years, destroyed more than half of it.

metadata.source: Historisch overzicht van de Dorpskerk te Cadzand
metadata.note: Fragment uit historisch document, opgenomen als facsimile. Tekst geïnterpreteerd vanuit oud-Nederlands.

title: First mention of the name Cadesant and the land ownership of the St-Baafsabdij.
text: The island of Cadzand is not mentioned before the year 1000. This may be related to the fact that important possessions on the island were owned by the Ghent St-Baafsabdij, of which there are not many written sources from the earliest times. The name Cadesant first appears in a charter, in which tithes (church taxes) from Wulpen and Cadesant were appropriated, which belonged to the church in Rodenburg (Aardenburg) and had been donated to the St-Baafsabdij by his predecessors.

metadata.source: Historisch overzicht van de Dorpskerk te Cadzand

title: Meaning of the name Cadzand: banks and dike construction, the oldest in Western Europe.
text: Cadesant indicates that there must have already been a clear system of dikes present here; the existing island was continuously expanded by surrounding one piece of marshland after another with a dike, thus creating a polder. The dike construction on the land of Cadzand is among the oldest in Western Europe.

metadata.source: Historisch overzicht van de Dorpskerk te Cadzand

title: Polders in Cadzand in 1177: Walter of Axel and Philip of Alsace
text: In 1177, the first written mention of polders on the island of Cadzand occurred. Knight Walter van Axel then relinquished his count's fief on Cadzand, which was spread out "super Val et super Grotenflit et in Kercpolre et Strinc et Bertenhuc et Sumpel" in favor of the St-Baafsabdij. A few years later, Count Philips van de Elzas expanded the land holdings of St-Baafs on Cadzand by 100 gemeten. This land was located, among other places, in "Sudpolre super Dondelinsvliet." One gemet corresponded to approximately one hectare.

metadata.source: Historisch overzicht van de Dorpskerk te Cadzand

title: The formation of the island of Zuidzande through polder development.
text: The island of Zuidzande was formed in the same way as the island of Cadzand: an old core surrounded by a large number of small polders.

metadata.source: Historisch overzicht van de Dorpskerk te Cadzand

title: Chapel on Wulpen (11th century) and the parish of St-Baafsabdij
text: On the island of Wulpen, there were at least two chapels at the end of the 11th century. These belonged to the parish of Rodenburg (Aardenburg) of the St-Baafsabdij.

metadata.source: Historisch overzicht van de Dorpskerk te Cadzand

title: Church in Cadzand around 1100 and the patronage rights of the St-Baafsabdij.
text: Probably, there was already a church on the island of Cadzand around 1100. The Mariakerk in Cadzand is also believed to have been founded by St-Baafs, as it held the patronage rights here: that is, the right to collect tithes and appoint a pastor when a church was established.

metadata.source: Historisch overzicht van de Dorpskerk te Cadzand

title: Spiritual care in Cadzand until 1231: request for a chaplain due to storm and crossing.
text: Until 1231, pastoral care on the island of Cadzand was provided by only one clergyman. From that year, there is a request from Cadzand to the abbot of St-Baafs to appoint a chaplain in addition to the pastor. It often happened that the pastor, when he had left the island for one reason or another, could not make the crossing for days due to storms or rough waters, and thus no spiritual assistance could be provided.

metadata.source: Historisch overzicht van de Dorpskerk te Cadzand

title: Four parishes on the island of Wulpen in the 13th century.
text: At the end of the 13th century, the island of Wulpen had four parishes: in Remboudsdorpe, in Brile (Avenkerke), in Westende-Wulpen (St-Precatuskerke), and in Ostende-Wulpen (St-Lambertus).

metadata.source: Historisch overzicht van de Dorpskerk te Cadzand

title: Ferry service between the islands: the St-Marie Ferry in Cadzand
text: The islands had a regular ferry service, both with the mainland and between each other. In Cadzand, there was the "St-Marie Veer," named after the patroness of the church of Cadzand. The names Veerhoek and Veerhoeve indicate that there have been various crossing points.

metadata.source: Historisch overzicht van de Dorpskerk te Cadzand

title: Island Wulpen was swallowed up in the 16th century; Cadzand was no longer an island in the 18th century.
text: The island of Wulpen was almost entirely swallowed by the sea in the 16th century, while the island of Cadzand gradually became connected to Groede and Zuidzande and in the 18th century to Oostburg, thus no longer being an island.

metadata.source: Historisch overzicht van de Dorpskerk te Cadzand

title: Construction history of the Mariakerk in Cadzand (1250-1325, Scheldt Gothic)
text: The Mariakerk is a two-aisled hall church; the southern aisle was likely built between 1250 and 1300; the northern aisle between 1300 and 1325. The style is primarily early Gothic (Scheldt Gothic), but the heavy columns are still Romanesque.

metadata.source: Historisch overzicht van de Dorpskerk te Cadzand

title: Sint-Lambertustoren as a beacon for sailors and mention in the sea atlas by Joris Carolus (1634)
text: The church had a tall, massive tower, the so-called Sint-Lambertustoren, which served as a beacon for sailors. Joris Carolus mentions in his sea atlas of 1634: "When (namely at the entrance of the Wielingen) the Castle of Sluis comes to a high, stout tower, standing on Cadzand west of Sluis, called Sint Lamberd, then one has passed the French Toll and may freely head east directly towards Vlissingen."

metadata.source: Historisch overzicht van de Dorpskerk te Cadzand

title: Invasions by the French and English in Cadzand (1300-1400)
text: Almost nothing is known about the medieval history of the church, but it can be assumed that the building suffered greatly from the invasions of the French and English, who repeatedly came to plunder the village of Cadzand and its surroundings in the years between 1300 and 1400, as well as from the civil wars.

metadata.source: Historisch overzicht van de Dorpskerk te Cadzand

title: Floods and poverty in the 14th-15th century: St. Elisabeth's Flood (1404) and storms.
text: The political unrest in the cities and the severe floods in the 14th and 15th centuries (such as the St. Elisabeth flood of 1404 and the storms of 1471, 1477, and 1497) caused great poverty in the countryside.

metadata.source: Historisch overzicht van de Dorpskerk te Cadzand

title: Civil War Flanders 1491: Maximilian of Austria and the Peace of Cadzand (1492)
text: During the civil war in Flanders, the island of Cadzand was captured in 1491 by troops of Maximilian of Austria to disrupt trade over the Zwin. This was accompanied by many acts of arson. In 1492, the peace of Cadzand was concluded.

metadata.source: Historisch overzicht van de Dorpskerk te Cadzand

title: Eighty Years' War: Spanish Occupation and Plundering Expeditions of the Geuzen
text: The church suffered no less during the Eighty Years' War, when the region was occupied by Spanish troops. The Geuzen of Vlissingen continuously conducted landings (raiding and plundering expeditions) in the Parma-occupied northern Flanders and everywhere they could "stole the lead from the church gutters to cast bullets for their artillery."

metadata.source: Historisch overzicht van de Dorpskerk te Cadzand
metadata.subsection: De Tachtigjarige Oorlog en de Reformatie

title: Catholicism holds its ground in Cadzand; Protestants' request in 1582; Parma recaptured Sluis in 1587.
text: Probably due to the significant Spanish influence in the region, the Roman Catholic faith remained strong in Cadzand for a long time. In 1582, the residents of Cadzand went to the Protestant classis in Breskens with a request for a pastor. After the occupation of Sluis in 1587 by Parma, all Flemish municipalities became "Spanish" again.

metadata.source: Historisch overzicht van de Dorpskerk te Cadzand
metadata.subsection: De Tachtigjarige Oorlog en de Reformatie

title: Prince Maurits conquers West Zeeuws-Vlaanderen in 1604; weekly preaching service via Sluis (1606)
text: In 1604, Prince Maurits conquered Philippine, IJzendijke, Aardenburg, and Sluis in one expedition, thereby bringing the entire area back under the Union of Utrecht. The church of Sluis temporarily took care of the interests of their fellow believers in Cadzand. The States-General decided in 1606 that there should be a sermon held weekly in Cadzand from Sluis. However, the turbulent Zwin often prevented the crossing.

metadata.source: Historisch overzicht van de Dorpskerk te Cadzand
metadata.subsection: De Tachtigjarige Oorlog en de Reformatie

title: The first own preacher, Marcus ab Halle, accepts service in 1609.
text: In 1607, the church was somewhat restored, and in 1609, the first own pastor, Marcus ab Halle, accepted his service.

metadata.source: Historisch overzicht van de Dorpskerk te Cadzand
metadata.subsection: De Tachtigjarige Oorlog en de Reformatie

title: Restoration 1610-1641: northern aisle restored; St-Baafsabdij pays as a tithe collector.
text: In 1610, the southern aisle was completely restored; the northern aisle remained in ruins until 1641, when an expansion of the church became necessary. The Vrije van Sluis then donated a stained glass window to the church. The restoration costs were largely borne by the St-Baafsabdij in Ghent, which was obligated to do so according to Flemish law as the tithe collector of the parish. This was a remarkable situation, when the Catholic mother church pays for the restoration of a church building that had transitioned to Protestantism.

metadata.source: Historisch overzicht van de Dorpskerk te Cadzand
metadata.subsection: De Tachtigjarige Oorlog en de Reformatie

title: Land Ownership of the Church of Cadzand 1610-1677: from 100 ha to Forced Sale
text: The church of Cadzand originally had a wealth of land ownership; in 1610 it amounted to nearly 100 hectares; by 1665 this had already shrunk to about 58 hectares. In 1677, all the lands had to be sold to pay off loans and overdue interest.

metadata.source: Historisch overzicht van de Dorpskerk te Cadzand
metadata.subsection: Grondbezit en afbraak van de toren

title: Demolition of the Sint-Lambertustoren in 1677; roof lantern and bell by Jan Burgerhuys (1611); sacristy in the rectory.
text: In 1677, the tower was in danger of completely collapsing and was demolished with the permission of Het Vrije van Sluis. A small roof turret was placed on the church, in which the bell cast by Jan Burgerhuys in Middelburg in 1611 was hung. The sacristy, covered with a Romanesque cross vault, was rebuilt but later separated from the church and incorporated into the rectory built in 1623, where it formed the central room of the pastor's residence.

metadata.source: Historisch overzicht van de Dorpskerk te Cadzand
metadata.subsection: Grondbezit en afbraak van de toren

title: West-Zeeuws-Vlaanderen as a colonized land: religious refugees from 1604
text: West Zeeuws-Vlaanderen has, over the centuries, received a strong influence from foreigners; it is largely a colonized land. From 1604 onwards, many refugees settled in these regions due to their faith: Anabaptists from Flanders, Salzburgers, but especially Huguenots and Protestants from Wallonia.

metadata.source: Historisch overzicht van de Dorpskerk te Cadzand

title: Huguenots establish Walloon-Reformed Congregation in Cadzand after the revocation of the Edict of Nantes (1685)
text: After the revocation of the Edict of Nantes in 1685, Huguenots from the vicinity of Calais came to Cadzand and established a Walloon Reformed Congregation, which was significantly increased in 1713 by fellow believers from the area around Lille, while in 1771 a few more came from the regions of Picardy, Champagne, and the area of Orléans.

metadata.source: Historisch overzicht van de Dorpskerk te Cadzand

title: The Waals-Hervormde Gemeente receives its own church in 1719 and merges into the Hervormd in 1809; a light chandelier as a legacy.
text: The Walloon Reformed Congregation held its religious services in the Reformed Church until 1719, when it acquired its own building. Due to integration with the local population, the Walloon Congregation merged into the Reformed congregation in 1809; the chandelier in the middle of the church is the only remnant of it. The presence of many French surnames in this region will always keep the history alive.

metadata.source: Historisch overzicht van de Dorpskerk te Cadzand

title: Hague Treaty 1795: State Flanders to the French Republic; Zeelandic Flanders from 1814.
text: At the Hague Treaty of 1795, Staats-Vlaanderen was ceded to the French Republic. In 1814, it was reunited with Holland and has since been called Zeeuws-Vlaanderen.

metadata.source: Historisch overzicht van de Dorpskerk te Cadzand

title: Roof renewal in 1821 with a subsidy from King Willem I; rectory demolished in 1894.
text: In 1821, the entire roof of the church had to be renewed; King Willem I granted a subsidy of one thousand guilders. In 1894, the rectory was demolished and replaced by a new one, resulting in the disappearance of the medieval sacristy.

metadata.source: Historisch overzicht van de Dorpskerk te Cadzand

title: Major restoration in 1929 by state architect Van Heeswijk; Renaissance turret added.
text: From 1929, the church was significantly restored by state architect Van Heeswijk. The walls of the church were brought back to their original height, and the capitals and bases of the pillars were restored. The wood carvings from the earlier benches, dating from 1650, were used to decorate the new benches. Instead of the small roof turret, the municipal authorities added an elegant Renaissance turret against the church.

metadata.source: Historisch overzicht van de Dorpskerk te Cadzand

title: Liberation 1944: shelling damages church; 18th-century stained glass windows lost.
text: During the liberation of West-Zeeuws-Vlaanderen in 1944, heavy shelling caused significant damage to the church building. The stained glass windows from the 18th century have all been lost.

metadata.source: Historisch overzicht van de Dorpskerk te Cadzand

title: The rectory was demolished in the 1950s; the church is protected by the Monument Care Foundation.
text: In the 1950s, the rectory was demolished and the garden took on its current appearance. The building is under the Foundation for Monument Care and will hopefully be preserved for our descendants for a long time thanks to this foundation, the municipality of Oostburg, and the local population.

metadata.source: Historisch overzicht van de Dorpskerk te Cadzand

title: Bibliographic sources of the booklet
text: Sources used in this booklet: Historical Geography of Western Zeeuws-Vlaanderen, parts I and II, by Dr. M.K.E. Gottschalk; History of the Reformed and Walloon Community in Cadzand; text fragment municipal archive Oostburg; Encarta '98 Encyclopedia; Winkler Prins Edition.

metadata.source: Historisch overzicht van de Dorpskerk te Cadzand

title: Foundation for the Preservation of the Village Church Cadzand: publication information and donation appeal (1998)
text: Publication of the Stichting Behoud Dorpskerk Cadzand, August 1998. A significant amount of money is needed to keep our church in good condition. Perhaps you would like to contribute to this. It is possible to transfer money to the following account: Rabobank Cadzand, account number 139407200, with the reference 'Behoud Dorpskerk Cadzand'.

metadata.source: Historisch overzicht van de Dorpskerk te Cadzand
